steps.title: Identify the missing piece
steps.content:
The research question asks whether perceived category similarity predicts purchase likelihood.

Grasby et al. already have a measure of actual purchase likelihood (the probability changes they calculated for each extended-brand pair), but they need a corresponding measure of how similar consumers perceive those categories to be.

steps.title: Match the design to the research question
steps.content:
Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair. directly measures perceived category similarity for each extended-brand pair and correlates it with the probability changes—the exact comparison needed to test whether category similarity predicts purchase behavior.

The other three options instead measure within-category similarity, brand recognition, and product cost respectively, none of which answer the research question.

That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
